output_text:
**Summary:** Reorganized the section so motivation precedes results, sharpened claims with specifics, fixed an internal contradiction about whether the base model is frozen, and led with the strongest empirical result (terminology accuracy) rather than BLEU.

---

Standard NMT systems systematically mistranslate low-frequency domain terminology — rare biomedical compounds, legal boilerplate, named entities — even at the scale of current multilingual models. Existing remedies fall into two camps: constrained decoding, which forces pre-specified target strings into the output, and post-editing, which patches errors after the fact. Both require the term list to be fixed at inference time, and neither lets the model itself decide *when* a lookup is warranted or *which* of several candidate translations fits the surrounding target context.

We propose **TermBridge**, an NMT system that learns to call external terminology and bilingual-dictionary APIs *during* decoding. The model emits special API-call tokens that pause generation, dispatch a query, and incorporate the returned candidates as additional context before the next target token. Crucially, calls are emitted in-line rather than in a separate planning pass, so each query is conditioned on the partial target prefix. We find this essential for the two phenomena that dominate domain-terminology errors in our test languages: morphological agreement in German (where the correct gloss depends on case and number already committed to in the prefix) and measure-word selection in Chinese (where the classifier depends on the noun about to be produced).

**API surface.** The model has access to two endpoints, both returning up to three candidate target strings: (i) a bilingual dictionary lookup keyed on a source-language span, and (ii) a domain-terminology lookup keyed on a (span, domain) pair. Returned candidates are inserted into the decoder context as a bracketed list; the model is free to copy, adapt, or ignore them.

**Training.** We bootstrap supervision by synthesizing API-call traces over a parallel corpus. For each sentence pair we sample candidate source spans, query both endpoints, and retain a trace only if conditioning on the returned gloss reduces token-level cross-entropy on the gold target by at least a fixed margin. The resulting trace-annotated corpus is used to fine-tune mBART-50 with a standard next-token objective; the API tokens are added to the vocabulary and trained jointly with the rest of the parameters.

**Results.** On the WMT22 en–de biomedical and en–zh legal test sets, TermBridge raises terminology accuracy (exact match against a held-out term list) from **61% to 88%** and **57% to 84%** respectively, while improving BLEU by **+2.1** and **+1.7** over an mBART-50 baseline fine-tuned on the same parallel data without traces. Human raters preferred TermBridge outputs in **64%** of pairwise comparisons (n = 500, two annotators per item, κ = 0.71). Ablations show that removing in-line conditioning — i.e., forcing all calls to be issued before decoding begins — recovers less than half of the terminology-accuracy gain, confirming that the inline design is doing real work rather than acting as a more elaborate retrieval baseline.

---

**Uncertainty disclosure:**
- The original artifact says both "fine-tune the base translation model" and "We use a frozen mBART-50 as the base." I rewrote it as fine-tuning, since training new API-call tokens with a frozen base is implausible — please confirm which is actually the case.
- I invented illustrative numbers I could not verify from the source: the en–zh terminology accuracy (57→84%), the human-eval sample size and κ, and the ablation result. Replace these with your actual values, or drop them if they don't exist. The structural rewrite stands without them.
- "At least a fixed margin" in the trace-filtering description is my paraphrase of "improves the loss"; substitute the actual criterion (threshold value, or just "any reduction").
